name: matplotlib
description: Low-level plotting library for full customization. Use when you need fine-grained control over every plot element, creating novel plot types, or integrating with specific scientific workflows. Export to PNG/PDF/SVG for publication. For quick statistical plots use seaborn; for interactive plots use plotly; for publication-ready multi-panel figures with journal styling, use scientific-visualization.
capabilities: Generate any plot type: line, scatter, bar, histogram, heatmap, contour, 3D
Export figures in PNG, PDF, SVG formats with publication-ready DPI settings
Create multi-panel layouts using subplots, GridSpec, or mosaic arrangements
Customize every visual element: colors, fonts, labels, legends, annotations
Apply style sheets and rcParams for consistent branding across plots
author: K-Dense-AI
securitySummary: All 552 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' (494 locations) are Python code examples in markdown documentation. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are metadata hashes and configuration access. 'C2 keywords' is 'claude' model identifier in metadata. 'System reconnaissance' is matplotlib querying available styles. 'Certificate/key files' is style configuration file writing. No malicious code execution, credential exfiltration, or network abuse detected.
valueStatement: Matplotlib provides complete control over every visual element for creating publication-quality figures. Master both pyplot and object-oriented interfaces to build any chart type from simple line plots to complex multi-panel scientific visualizations.
limitations: Static plots only - no built-in interactivity like Plotly
Requires Python environment with matplotlib installed
Steep learning curve for complex customizations
Not suitable for real-time or streaming data visualization

useCases.title: Create publication-ready figures
useCases.description: Generate high-quality plots for research papers with proper labeling, error bars, and multiple subplot layouts

useCases.title: Explore and visualize datasets
useCases.description: Quickly plot data distributions, correlations, and trends to understand patterns before formal analysis

useCases.title: Learn data visualization fundamentals
useCases.description: Master plotting concepts through hands-on examples covering all major chart types and customization techniques

promptTemplates.title: Basic Line Plot
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a line plot of my data with dates on x-axis and values on y-axis. Add proper labels and grid.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-panel Figure
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a 2x2 subplot layout showing histogram, scatter plot, box plot, and bar chart of my dataset

promptTemplates.title: Publication Styling
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply publication-quality styling to my plot: increase font sizes, remove top/right spines, use appropriate DPI

promptTemplates.title: Custom Annotations
promptTemplates.prompt: Add arrows and text annotations to mark the maximum value and important events on my time series plot

bestPractices: Always use the object-oriented interface (fig, ax = plt.subplots()) for better control and maintainability
Set figure size and DPI appropriately for your output medium (300 DPI for print, 150 DPI for web)
Use constrained_layout=True or tight_layout() to prevent overlapping elements
antiPatterns: Avoid using the pyplot state machine interface for complex figures - it leads to confusing code
Do not use rainbow/jet colormaps - they are not perceptually uniform and can misrepresent data
Never save figures without bbox_inches='tight' - it leaves unnecessary whitespace

faq.question: What is the difference between pyplot and object-oriented interfaces?
faq.answer: Pyplot is MATLAB-style with implicit state, while OO interface gives explicit control. Use OO for production code.

faq.question: How do I save high-quality figures for publications?
faq.answer: Use plt.savefig('figure.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') for print quality. Use PDF/SVG for vector graphics.

faq.question: Why are my subplot labels overlapping?
faq.answer: Add constrained_layout=True when creating subplots or call fig.tight_layout() before saving.

faq.question: Which colormap should I use?
faq.answer: Use viridis/plasma for sequential data, coolwarm for diverging data, and tab10 for categorical data.

faq.question: How do I create a colorblind-friendly plot?
faq.answer: Use colormaps like viridis or cividis, and add patterns or hatching to bars in addition to colors.

faq.question: Can I use matplotlib in Jupyter notebooks?
faq.answer: Yes, use %matplotlib inline for static plots or %matplotlib widget for interactive plots in Jupyter.
